Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic even though I put in a lot of hours. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings while doing other stuff around my place, since I learn best by hearing things. I'd have these marathon sessions where I'd cram a ton of content, but I wasn't great about spacing it out evenly. Sometimes I'd be studying at 2am, other times mid-afternoon—it really depended on when I could focus through the anxiety.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally that week. I'd have stretches where I was super consistent, then other times where I'd barely touch anything for days. Deadlines definitely drove my intensity—I'd ramp up hard when something was coming due. The anxiety made it hard to stick to a regular schedule because some days I just couldn't bring myself to start.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered there might be something due or when I wanted to catch up on lecture recordings. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed any announcements because the anxiety would nag at me. Other times it was peer discussions that pulled me back in—seeing notifications that someone had replied to something. I wasn't using any edutech apps to remind me, so it was mostly self-motivated or panic-driven.
Q4: Yeah, there were periods where logging in became automatic, especially when I got into a groove with the video lectures. I'd wake up, make coffee, and put on a lecture like it was a podcast. The discussion boards also pulled me in regularly once I started interacting with a few classmates. But it took a while to build that habit, and it would break easily if life got complicated.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the concepts that way. The discussion boards were also surprisingly helpful because hearing other students' perspectives filled in gaps I didn't realize I had. Anything I could listen to while doing other tasks felt most valuable because of my limited resources and busy schedule.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read through them—reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also skipped because they felt tedious when I could just re-listen to lectures instead. If it wasn't audio-based, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to the exams or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because I could hear different viewpoints and it made concepts click. Anything that felt like busywork or repetitive exercises that didn't add new understanding—those felt like a waste when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford to engage with, even when they seemed interesting. Required stuff got done because it had to, but optional things I'd only do if I had extra mental bandwidth that week. Sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting, but the anxiety about completing required work always took priority. It's frustrating because I probably missed out on some good learning opportunities.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the audio from lectures and listen on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used free YouTube videos and podcasts on similar topics to supplement what we were learning. My notes were all handwritten because I retain information better when I write while listening, but none of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were tons of times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd have a lecture downloaded and listen to it three or four times over a few days while walking around. I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was in the same course over the phone. Sometimes my best studying happened at a coffee shop with printed notes and zero internet connection because I couldn't afford consistent data.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video once, download the audio, and then never touch the LMS version again even though I was studying that content for days. Same with any PDFs I actually did use—I'd download them once and work from the file. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material a dozen times offline. It was more practical given my limited resources.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming even though I wanted to engage. There were weeks where just opening my laptop felt like too much, so I'd avoid the LMS entirely. Financial stress also hit hard sometimes—worrying about rent made it nearly impossible to focus on coursework. The mindfulness exercises helped me push through some of those moments, but not always.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with money stress, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected even if I was still trying to study in other ways. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate. I'd end up in this cycle where falling behind increased my anxiety, which made it even harder to catch up.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too anxious about other stuff. Sometimes I'd log in and click around just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions were more about managing guilt than actual engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge wall I couldn't climb over. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow often looked the same. It's weird because I could ace exams, but assignments felt like this different beast that my brain couldn't handle. I think the open-ended nature of them triggered more anxiety than timed tests.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to actually submit work. I'd have this burst of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing through. Without a deadline, I'd drift and keep pushing things off. The LMS probably showed these spikes of activity clustered around due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could overcome the paralysis.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that area for a while. When instructors responded to discussion posts, it made me feel more connected and likely to participate again. But if I didn't get feedback, I'd start questioning whether my effort even mattered.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and video content organized logically made it easier to follow along. But the flexibility also let me fall behind because there wasn't enough external pressure until deadlines hit. I wish there had been more frequent small checkpoints to keep me on track. The structure worked for people with better self-discipline than me.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement way easier for me as an auditory learner. Being able to adjust playback speed and rewatch sections was huge. What made it harder was the amount of reading expected—I'd see a long PDF and immediately feel defeated. Also, some modules felt disconnected from each other, which made it hard to see the bigger picture and stay motivated.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was in the LMS a lot, watching videos, checking discussions, but my assignment completion was terrible. For me, it was the anxiety creating a gap between consuming content and producing work. I understood the material—my exam scores prove that—but translating that into submitted assignments felt like a completely different skill that my brain resisted.
Q21: That also kind of reflects my experience in a different way. Some of my best learning happened completely offline, so my LMS activity didn't capture the actual studying I did. If someone downloaded everything early and studied from their notes, they might look disengaged online but actually be super prepared. The LMS only sees one slice of how students actually learn.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually absorbing content or just letting a video play in the background. They also miss the emotional and mental state behind the activity—whether you're engaged or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even when I'm not actively studying. For me, engagement happened most during discussions with peers and while listening to lectures that made things click. It's less about time spent and more about mental investment in actually learning something.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while still being genuinely interested in the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or discussing concepts with friends outside the platform. It also can't show that my low assignment completion wasn't about not caring—it was about a mental barrier I couldn't always overcome. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the data.
